narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd hoped, landing at a 44% chance of not passing. Picture it like a story of your academic journey so far: your Year 3 grades placed you in the 2nd decile at school, and that turned out to be the biggest signal pulling toward difficulty ahead, as it suggests the challenges you faced later in your studies might carry over to the intense bar prep. Right behind that was your LSAT score of 32.3, which, while solid for many, didn't quite stand out in the way the model looks for to predict bar success. Your Year 1 decile of 2 added to that early pattern, painting a picture of consistent hurdles in a competitive law environment. Then there's your middle family income bracket, which the model linked to added pressures that sometimes make the road tougher without extra buffers. Your background as a Black female student factored in too, reflecting broader trends the model has seen in bar passage rates. Your undergrad GPA of 2.7 and choice to go part-time while studying wove into this narrative as quieter but steady influences toward that riskier outlook. It's not that any one piece defined you—it's how they all combined to tell the model a story of an uphill battle ahead, one where the odds tipped just enough against passing the bar to pause your application. This isn't the end of your story, though; many have rewritten theirs by targeting those early grade patterns or LSAT edges. You've got resilience in your profile—your passion for law shines through—and with focused steps, you can shift this script. Hang in there; we're rooting for your next chapter.
